ontology.foodon_gap_note: FoodOn has a general oolong tea beverage class (FOODON_03311171). No specific osmanthus oolong with honey subclass exists; closest_parent used.
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 code AN0EX covers oolong tea as a category. No specific osmanthus-flavoured or honey-added variant exists in FoodEx2; closest_parent used.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: SNOMED CT 227313005 covers oolong tea as a substance. No osmanthus oolong with honey specific code exists; closest_parent used.

serving.natural_language_portion: One standard serving is approximately 350 ml (350 g) of osmanthus oolong tea with honey, as typically served in a takeaway cup at Taiwanese-style tea shops in Singapore.

nutrient_source.title: Tea, oolong, brewed, prepared with tap water (USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy #14366) combined with Honey, raw (USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy #19304), proportionally weighted for a lightly sweetened oolong tea with honey
nutrient_source.organization: USDA Agricultural Research Service, FoodData Central
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: No direct USDA or HPB entry exists for osmanthus oolong tea with honey as a prepared beverage. Values are estimated by combining USDA SR Legacy data for brewed oolong tea (FDC #14366, ~1 kcal/100 ml) with a proportional contribution from honey (~304 kcal/100 g) at an estimated 3–4% honey addition by weight (approximately 10–14 g honey per 350 ml serving), yielding approximately 12–15 kcal per 100 ml of the final beverage. Osmanthus flowers contribute negligible macronutrients at typical infusion quantities. Values are rounded conservatively. Sodium is near zero for brewed tea. Sugar reflects honey contribution.

gi_source.title: Honey, GI value 35–58 (mean ~55 for raw honey); oolong tea contributes negligible glycemic carbohydrates. Composite GI estimated from honey contribution at ~3.5% dilution in brewed tea.
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement exists for osmanthus oolong tea with honey as a prepared beverage. Brewed oolong tea itself contains negligible glycemic carbohydrates (GI effectively 1, low). Honey has a published GI of approximately 35–58 in the University of Sydney GI Database (mean ~55 for raw honey, lower for some floral honeys). At approximately 3–4% honey by weight in the final beverage, the effective GI of the drink is substantially diluted. A conservative composite GI of 35 is assigned (low category), reflecting the dominant low-GI tea base with a modest honey contribution. GL per 350 g serving = (35 × 11.55 g available carbohydrate) / 100 = approximately 4.0, rounded to 3.8.

hypertension.key_factor: Very low sodium content; tea polyphenols may support vascular health
hypertension.guidance: With approximately 2 mg sodium per 100 ml and no added salt, osmanthus oolong tea with honey is suitable for individuals managing hypertension, though the honey addition contributes a small caloric and sugar load that should be accounted for in overall dietary planning.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Honey-derived free sugars contribute approximately 11 g of sugar per 350 ml serving
type2_diabetes.guidance: Individuals with Type 2 Diabetes should be aware that the honey in this beverage contributes approximately 11 g of free sugars per serving; oolong tea polyphenols may modestly support glycaemic regulation, but portion control and frequency of consumption should be discussed with a dietitian.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Negligible saturated fat and sodium; oolong tea polyphenols have antioxidant properties
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Osmanthus oolong tea with honey contains no saturated fat or cholesterol and very low sodium, making it a low cardiovascular-risk beverage; regular consumption of oolong tea has been associated with modest improvements in LDL oxidation in observational studies, though the honey addition should be moderated in individuals with dyslipidaemia.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Osmanthus Oolong Tea with Honey is a lightly sweetened floral tea beverage made by infusing oolong tea leaves with dried or fresh osmanthus flowers (Osmanthus fragrans) and finishing with a measured addition of honey. It is served at Taiwanese-style specialty tea shops, including CHICHA San Chen in Singapore, and is popular across East and Southeast Asia. The drink presents a delicate, layered aroma combining the roasted, semi-oxidised character of oolong tea with the sweet, apricot-like floral notes of osmanthus blossoms, rounded by the mild sweetness and subtle complexity of honey. The texture is light and watery, similar to brewed tea, with no creaminess or body from milk. Nutritionally, the beverage is very low in calories (approximately 13 kcal per 100 ml), contains negligible protein and fat, and derives its modest carbohydrate content (approximately 3.3 g per 100 ml) almost entirely from the honey addition. Sodium is near zero. Oolong tea is a rich source of polyphenols including catechins and theaflavins, which have been studied for antioxidant, anti-inflammatory, and modest glycaemic-regulatory effects. Osmanthus flowers contribute trace phytochemicals and aromatic compounds. Honey provides free sugars (predominantly fructose and glucose) and trace minerals. Clinically, this beverage is low risk for hypertension and cardiovascular disease due to its negligible sodium and fat content, but individuals with Type 2 Diabetes should monitor the free sugar contribution from honey. The glycaemic index is estimated at approximately 35 (low), reflecting the dominant low-GI tea base with a diluted honey contribution. This beverage is vegetarian, gluten-free, and dairy-free, but not vegan due to the honey content. It is commonly served in 350–500 ml takeaway cups at a price point of approximately SGD 6.00 in Singapore.

confidence_reason: No direct entry for osmanthus oolong tea with honey exists in HPB Singapore Foodo, USDA FoodData Central, or any other approved primary source as a prepared beverage. Nutrient values are estimated by proportional combination of USDA SR Legacy data for brewed oolong tea and raw honey at a realistic dilution ratio. GI is an analogous estimate based on University of Sydney GI Database honey values, adjusted for dilution. Confidence is therefore medium rather than high.
notes: 1. protein_g is naturally zero: brewed oolong tea contains negligible protein, and the honey contribution at 3–4% dilution yields sub-0.05 g protein per 100 ml, rounded to 0.0. Confirmed zero per USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy #14366 (oolong tea, brewed). 2. fat_g is naturally zero: oolong tea and honey contain no fat; confirmed zero per USDA FoodData Central. 3. saturated_fat_g, monounsaturated_fat_g, polyunsaturated_fat_g, trans_fat_g, cholesterol_mg, and dietary_fibre_g are all 0.0 as naturally zero for this brewed tea beverage with honey. 4. glycemic_index set to 35 (low category) as an analogous estimate; the tea base itself would be effectively GI 1 (no glycemic carbohydrates), but the honey addition raises the composite GI to approximately 35 at the dilution used. gi_category is low. 5. Vitamin A, B-vitamins (B1, B2, B3, B6, B12), folate, vitamin D, vitamin E, phosphorus, magnesium, zinc, and selenium are all -1 as these micronutrients are not reliably reported for brewed oolong tea with honey in the USDA FoodData Central database at the level of precision required; values are below detection or not reported in the source. 6. Serving size of 350 g is an estimate based on standard takeaway cup sizes at Taiwanese tea shops in Singapore. 7. The food is not vegan due to honey content. 8. Osmanthus flowers contribute negligible macronutrients at typical infusion quantities and are not separately quantified in the nutrient profile. 9. Price of SGD 6.00 is preserved from the field survey data source record (GD001376). 10. The original input food_name 'Osmanthus Oolong Tea + Honey水仙桂花 + 百花香蜜' has been normalised to the canonical English name 'Osmanthus Oolong Tea with Honey'.

improvement_actions_taken: Normalised food_name from menu-style input to canonical English 'Osmanthus Oolong Tea with Honey'.
Added Chinese simplified, Chinese traditional, Malay, Tamil, and Hindi local names to satisfy G6 multilingual compliance for Chinese/Singaporean cuisine.
Estimated per_100g nutrient values from USDA SR Legacy data for brewed oolong tea and raw honey at realistic dilution, with full methodology documented in nutrient_source and notes.
Justified protein_g=0.0 and fat_g=0.0 as naturally zero with confirmed zero phrase per USDA FoodData Central.
Set glycemic_index to 35 (numeric > 0, low category) as analogous estimate from University of Sydney GI Database honey values adjusted for dilution; documented in gi_source and notes.
Documented all -1 sentinel values (vitamin A, B-vitamins, folate, vitamin D, vitamin E, phosphorus, magnesium, zinc, selenium) in notes as not reported in source database.
Verified aliases_by_language and flat aliases list are perfectly consistent — all strings in aliases_by_language appear verbatim in aliases.
Set all ontology match_type values to allowed values only (closest_parent) with gap notes.
Added health_context for all three required conditions with specific clinical guidance sentences.
Set human_condition_name to 'Type 2 Diabetes' in the type2_diabetes block.
